message:
Align robot JSON schemas with wa-* bead specs

- wa-robot-approve.json: Clarify envelope error pattern, add dry_run field,
  document stable E_* error codes, remove conflicting error_reason in data
- wa-robot-workflow-status.json: Add paused status, step_name, elapsed_ms,
  last_step_result fields from wa-7dd spec
- wa-robot-workflow-abort.json: Add pane_id, aborted_at_step, step_name,
  forced, cleanup_performed fields from wa-55y spec; add paused to status enum
- wa-robot-workflow-list.json: Add agent_types, step_count, requires_approval,
  can_abort, dependencies fields from wa-35l spec

Closes duplicate bd-* beads in favor of more comprehensive wa-* beads.
Updates bd-h1ai EPIC and bd-39wn with detailed testing requirements.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1dnn","title":"Add wa robot why command","description":"## Goal
Add `wa robot why <code>` command for agents to understand error codes and policy denials.

## Rationale
When agents receive errors like `robot.policy_denied` or `robot.require_approval`, they need programmatic access to explanations and remediation steps.

## Implementation
1. Add `RobotCommands::Why { code: String }`
2. Query `wa_core::explanations::get_explanation(code)`
3. Return JSON with: scenario, brief, detailed, suggestions, see_also
4. Create wa-robot-why.json schema

## Response Format
\`\`\`json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "code": "deny.alt_screen",
    "scenario": "Send denied because alt-screen is active",
    "brief": "Pane is in full-screen mode (vim, less, etc.)",
    "detailed": "The pane is currently displaying...",
    "suggestions": ["Exit the full-screen application first", ...],
    "see_also": ["wa policy", "wa status --pane <id>"]
  }
}
\`\`\`

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa robot why deny.alt_screen` returns explanation
- `wa robot why unknown.code` returns error with available codes

-{"id":"bd-39wn","title":"wa robot approve: submit approval codes in robot mode","description":"# wa robot approve

## Purpose
Allow agents to submit approval codes programmatically through robot mode. This mirrors the human `wa approve` command but with JSON output suitable for automation.

## Command Interface
```bash
# Submit an approval code
wa robot approve <code>

# Submit with explicit pane context (for fingerprint validation)
wa robot approve <code> --pane 3
```

## JSON Output Schema
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "approve",
  "data": {
    "code": "a1b2c3",
    "valid": true,
    "action_kind": "SendText",
    "pane_id": 3,
    "expires_at": 1737300000000,
    "consumed_at": 1737296400000,
    "action_fingerprint": "sha256:abc123..."
  }
}
```

## Error Cases
- E_APPROVAL_NOT_FOUND: No approval with given code
- E_APPROVAL_EXPIRED: Approval code has expired
- E_APPROVAL_CONSUMED: Approval already used
- E_FINGERPRINT_MISMATCH: Action changed since approval issued
- E_WRONG_PANE: Approval was for different pane

## Implementation Notes
- Query ApprovalStore for the approval token
- Validate workspace scope, expiry, and fingerprint
- Mark approval as consumed on success
- Record in audit trail

## Relationship to Human Command
- `wa approve` (wa-nu4.3.2.12): Human-friendly CLI with colored output
- `wa robot approve` (this bead): JSON output for agent consumption
- Both use the same underlying ApprovalStore

## Testing
- Unit tests: JSON serialization, error code stability
- Integration: Approval consumption works correctly
- E2E: Agent can request approval, human grants via `wa approve`, agent confirms via `wa robot approve`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Approval submission returns structured JSON
- [ ] All error cases return stable error codes
- [ ] Approval consumption is audited
- [ ] JSON validates against schema
- [ ] Works with existing ApprovalStore infrastructure","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-22T18:51:09.929485182Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-22T18:51:21.759632515Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-robot","area-safety","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-39wn","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-22T18:51:09.973556779Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-39wn","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.7.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-22T18:51:21.759553917Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-39wn","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.8.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-22T18:51:18.411232480Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
+{"id":"bd-39wn","title":"wa robot approve: submit approval codes in robot mode","description":"# wa robot approve

## Purpose
Allow agents to submit approval codes programmatically through robot mode. This mirrors the human `wa approve` command but with JSON output suitable for automation.

## Command Interface
```bash
# Submit an approval code
wa robot approve <code>

# Submit with explicit pane context (for fingerprint validation)
wa robot approve <code> --pane 3

# Check approval status without consuming (dry-run)
wa robot approve <code> --dry-run
```

## JSON Output Schema
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "command": "approve",
  "data": {
    "code": "a1b2c3",
    "valid": true,
    "action_kind": "SendText",
    "pane_id": 3,
    "expires_at": 1737300000000,
    "consumed_at": 1737296400000,
    "action_fingerprint": "sha256:abc123..."
  }
}
```

## Error Cases (Stable Codes)
- E_APPROVAL_NOT_FOUND: No approval with given code
- E_APPROVAL_EXPIRED: Approval code has expired
- E_APPROVAL_CONSUMED: Approval already used
- E_FINGERPRINT_MISMATCH: Action changed since approval issued
- E_WRONG_PANE: Approval was for different pane
- E_WRONG_WORKSPACE: Approval was for different workspace

## Implementation Notes
- Query ApprovalStore for the approval token
- Validate workspace scope, expiry, and fingerprint
- Mark approval as consumed on success (unless --dry-run)
- Record in audit trail

## Relationship to Human Command
- `wa approve` (wa-nu4.3.2.12): Human-friendly CLI with colored output
- `wa robot approve` (this bead): JSON output for agent consumption
- Both use the same underlying ApprovalStore

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
```rust
#[test]
fn test_approve_valid_code() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_approval(...);
    
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code]);
    assert!(output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    assert!(output["data"]["valid"].as_bool().unwrap());
    assert!(output["data"]["consumed_at"].is_number());
}

#[test]
fn test_approve_expired_code() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_expired_approval();
    
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code]);
    assert!(!output["ok"].as_bool().unwrap());
    assert_eq!(output["error"]["code"], "E_APPROVAL_EXPIRED");
}

#[test]
fn test_approve_consumed_code() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_approval(...);
    store.consume(&code);
    
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code]);
    assert_eq!(output["error"]["code"], "E_APPROVAL_CONSUMED");
}

#[test]
fn test_approve_wrong_pane() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_approval_for_pane(5);
    
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code, "--pane", "3"]);
    assert_eq!(output["error"]["code"], "E_WRONG_PANE");
}

#[test]
fn test_approve_dry_run_does_not_consume() {
    let store = setup_approval_store();
    let code = store.create_approval(...);
    
    execute_robot(&["approve", &code, "--dry-run"]);
    
    // Code should still be valid
    let output = execute_robot(&["approve", &code]);
    assert!(output["data"]["valid"].as_bool().unwrap());
}
```

### E2E Test
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# e2e_approval_flow.sh
set -euo pipefail
LOG="$ARTIFACT_DIR/approval_flow.log"
log() { echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] $*" | tee -a "$LOG"; }

log "=== Approval Flow E2E ==="

# 1. Try to send something that requires approval
log "Attempting send that requires approval..."
SEND_RESULT=$(wa robot send 0 "test" 2>&1 || true)
log "Send result: $SEND_RESULT"

# 2. Extract approval code from RequireApproval response
CODE=$(echo "$SEND_RESULT" | jq -r '.data.allow_once_code // empty')
if [ -z "$CODE" ]; then
    log "No approval code in response, skipping approval test"
    exit 0
fi

log "Got approval code: $CODE"

# 3. Check with dry-run first
log "Dry-run approve..."
DRY=$(wa robot approve "$CODE" --dry-run)
log "Dry-run result: $DRY"
echo "$DRY" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: dry-run"; exit 1; }

# 4. Actually approve
log "Approving..."
APPROVE=$(wa robot approve "$CODE")
log "Approve result: $APPROVE"
echo "$APPROVE" | jq -e '.ok == true' || { log "FAIL: approve"; exit 1; }

# 5. Verify it was consumed
log "Verifying consumed..."
RETRY=$(wa robot approve "$CODE" 2>&1 || true)
log "Retry result: $RETRY"
echo "$RETRY" | jq -e '.error.code == "E_APPROVAL_CONSUMED"' || { log "FAIL: not consumed"; exit 1; }

log "=== PASS: approval_flow ==="
```

```rust
}
```

### Scenario: Workflow Run → Status → Abort Flow
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# e2e_workflow_lifecycle.sh

set -euo pipefail
LOG_FILE="$ARTIFACT_DIR/workflow_lifecycle.log"
PANE_ID="${TEST_PANE_ID:-0}"

log() { echo "[$(date -Iseconds)] $*" | tee -a "$LOG_FILE"; }

log "Starting workflow lifecycle E2E test"

# Start a workflow
log "Starting workflow..."
RUN_OUTPUT=$(wa robot workflow run handle_compaction "$PANE_ID" --dry-run 2>&1 || true)
log "Run output: $RUN_OUTPUT"

# Extract execution ID (if available)
EXEC_ID=$(echo "$RUN_OUTPUT" | jq -r '.data.execution_id // empty')

if [ -n "$EXEC_ID" ]; then
    log "Got execution ID: $EXEC_ID"
    
    # Check status
    log "Checking status..."
    STATUS_OUTPUT=$(wa robot workflow status "$EXEC_ID")
    log "Status output: $STATUS_OUTPUT"
    
    STATUS=$(echo "$STATUS_OUTPUT" | jq -r '.data.status')
    log "Current status: $STATUS"
    
    # Abort if running
    if [ "$STATUS" = "running" ] || [ "$STATUS" = "waiting" ]; then
        log "Aborting workflow..."
        ABORT_OUTPUT=$(wa robot workflow abort "$EXEC_ID" --reason "E2E test cleanup")
        log "Abort output: $ABORT_OUTPUT"
        
        ABORTED=$(echo "$ABORT_OUTPUT" | jq -r '.data.aborted')
        [ "$ABORTED" = "true" ] || { log "FAIL: abort failed"; exit 1; }
        log "✓ Workflow aborted successfully"
    fi
fi

log "✓ PASS: workflow lifecycle E2E test"
```

```rust
}
```

```bash
fi
```

 {"id":"wa-01x","title":"Version compatibility check: detect incompatible wa/database combinations","description":"# Task: Version Compatibility Check

## Goal
Prevent users from accidentally running incompatible wa versions against their database.

## Scenarios
1. **Newer wa, older DB**: Migration needed (usually safe)
2. **Older wa, newer DB**: DANGEROUS - wa cannot read newer schema
3. **Major version jump**: May require manual intervention

## Implementation

### Database Version Tracking
Track schema_version, min_compatible_wa, created_by_wa, created_at in wa_meta table.

### Startup Check
On open, verify:
- current_wa >= min_compatible_wa (else: WaTooOld error)
- schema_version <= supported_schema (else: SchemaTooNew error)
- schema_version == supported_schema (else: MigrationNeeded)

### User-Facing Messages
Old wa + new DB: Clear error with upgrade instructions
New wa + old DB: Offer migration with backup

## Testing
- Unit tests: version comparison logic
- Integration: simulate version mismatches
- E2E: upgrade/downgrade scenarios

## Acceptance Criteria
- Old wa refuses to open newer DB with clear error
- New wa offers migration for older DB
- Version info tracked in database metadata
- Helpful upgrade instructions provided","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:58:00.500288986Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T20:45:47.047519125Z","compaction_level":0,"labels":["area-cli","area-storage","area-tests","area-ux","phase-1","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-19T20:45:47.047453772Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-19T20:45:44.971218440Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:58:19.090737493Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.3.2.11","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:55:36.964558253Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-01x","depends_on_id":"wa-rnf.5","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:55:36.203121200Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"wa-4vx.7.7","title":"Quick-start mode (no args) + `wa robot quick-start` output tuned for agents","description":"# Task: Robot quick-start output

## Goal
When invoked with no args (or via explicit command), output a dense, agent-friendly quick start.

This is a critical UX surface for agents:
- it teaches the stable contract (envelope + schemas)
- it reduces “guessing” about workspace/config
- it points to the safest default actions first

## Invocation
Support at least one (either is fine; both is ideal):
- `wa robot` (no subcommand) → quick-start
- `wa robot quick-start` → quick-start

## Output requirements
- Must be **stable JSON** (robot envelope) and validated against schemas (`wa-4vx.7.10`).
- Must be minimal tokens but still actionable.
- Must include:
  - a short description of wa robot mode
  - the canonical global flags:
    - `--workspace` / `WA_WORKSPACE`
    - `--config`
  - the “core loop” for an agent:
    1) `wa robot state`
    2) pick `pane_id`
    3) `wa robot get-text` / `wa robot search`
    4) `wa robot send --dry-run` (safety-first)
    5) `wa robot send` (only when policy allows)
  - references to safety expectations:
    - sends are policy-gated
    - `--dry-run` exists
    - how RequireApproval is surfaced (and how a human can approve)

## Design notes
- Treat this as a *structured help payload*, not a prose wall.
- Prefer a JSON structure like:
  - `commands: [{name, args, summary, examples[]}]`
  - `tips: [...]`
  - `error_handling: { common_codes: [...] }`

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - output validates against the quick-start schema
  - output includes the required command list and flags
  - output remains “small enough” (token/byte budget) so it’s usable in agent loops
- E2E:
  - runner can invoke the command and capture artifacts (stdout JSON + stderr logs)

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa robot` (no subcommand) and/or `wa robot quick-start` returns a stable JSON payload that teaches an agent how to use wa safely.
- The output is schema-validated and regression-tested (snapshot or schema validation + key field asserts).

-{"id":"wa-4vx.7.8","title":"Robot mode tests: envelope stability, error codes, command outputs (synthetic)","description":"# Task: Robot mode tests

## Goal
Prevent breaking changes in robot schemas and error codes.

Robot mode is the primary API surface for other tools/agents. A minor formatting change can break automation.

## Coverage
### Envelope stability
- Assert the JSON envelope contains the expected top-level fields (and only intentional additions):
  - ok
  - data (on success)
  - error (on failure)
  - hint (optional)
  - elapsed_ms
  - version
  - now

### Error code stability
- For common error cases, assert:
  - error.code is stable
  - error.message is actionable
  - hint is present when it improves remediation

### Command-level contract tests (incremental)
- Start with the phase-1 robot commands:
  - state
  - get-text
  - search
  - events
  - send
  - wait-for
- Extend this suite as new robot subcommands land in later phases:
  - workflow
  - accounts / accounts-refresh
  - rules list/test
  - **reservations / reserve / release**

## Schema approach
- Validate outputs against versioned JSON schemas (wa-4vx.7.10) in addition to snapshots.
- Snapshots are still useful for human review (especially for error payloads), but schema validation is the real guardrail.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests fail if:
  - a required field is removed/renamed
  - an error code changes
  - output becomes non-JSON or non-deterministic


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Include a “schema + snapshot mismatch” test to ensure both systems are actually running (not silently skipped).
  - Add at least one test per command that asserts deterministic field ordering (or explicitly documents if ordering is not guaranteed).

  → ○ wa-4vx.7.10: Robot JSON schemas: versioned envelope + per-command outputs, validated in tests ● P0

 {"id":"wa-4vx.7.9","title":"[Robot] `wa robot wait-for` (pattern wait with timeout, robust errors)","description":"# Task: wa robot wait-for

## Goal
Provide a dedicated robot command for waiting until a pane satisfies a condition without sending input.

This is a core building block for:
- agent orchestration loops (avoid sleeping blindly)
- workflows (shared wait logic)
- tests (deterministic sync points)

## Behavior
Command:
- wa robot wait-for <pane_id> "<pattern>" [--timeout-secs N] [--tail N] [--regex]

Semantics:
- Repeatedly poll the pane tail using the same underlying pane text acquisition as wa robot get-text.
- Default matcher is plain substring.
- Optional --regex enables regex matcher (must be explicit).
- Return success when matched.
- Return a stable error when timeout occurs.

## Workspace/config
- Honors global flags:
  - --workspace / WA_WORKSPACE
  - --config (optional)

## Implementation notes
- Use the shared PaneWaiter (wa-4vx.2.6) so robot, workflows, and send verification have identical polling/backoff semantics.
- Avoid logging full pane content.

## Error model (stable)
- WA-ROBOT-PANE-NOT-FOUND
- WA-ROBOT-TIMEOUT
- WA-ROBOT-GET-TEXT-FAILED

## Logging
- Log at INFO when:
  - starting wait (pane_id, timeout)
  - success (elapsed)
  - timeout

## Testing
- Unit tests with a synthetic pane/text source:
  - returns success when the pattern appears
  - returns timeout with stable error envelope
  - regex mode requires explicit flag

## Acceptance Criteria
- With a synthetic fixture, wait-for returns success when the pattern appears.
- Timeout returns a stable error envelope with elapsed_ms and a remediation hint.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.8","title":"[EPIC] Safety & policy engine (capability gates, rate limits, redaction)","description":"# Safety & policy engine

## Goal
Ensure wa never becomes a liability by enforcing deterministic safety constraints before any action that mutates state.

## Why this is critical
We are automating typing into terminals running powerful tools. A single wrong send can cause data loss.
We therefore treat action authorization as a first-class subsystem.

## Design requirements
- **Capability-based gates** (deterministic):
  - never send into alt-screen
  - require prompt-active for SendText where possible (OSC 133)
  - block SendText if output gap indicates uncertain state
- **Rate limiting** per pane and per action kind
- **Approval modes**:
  - interactive: prompt user for approvals
  - robot/mcp: return `RequireApproval` as an error with a clear reason
- **Secret redaction** for audit logs and outputs

## Deliverables
- `ActionKind` classification (SendText/Spawn/Split/BrowserAuth/WriteFile/ExecuteWorkflow/etc.)
- `PolicyEngine` evaluation:
  - allow/deny/require-approval
  - rule table loaded from config
- `Redactor`:
  - recognize API key patterns, tokens, passwords
  - ensure secrets never appear in logs or robot output
- Integration points:
  - all sends and workflows must call policy authorize

## Acceptance
- Attempting SendText while pane is AltScreen or CommandRunning is denied.
- Policy logs include redacted input.
- Robot mode returns stable error codes for denied actions.



## Success Criteria
- All input injection paths are policy-gated (robot/human/workflow) with safe defaults.
- Policy decisions are explainable (stable reasons + rule ids) and auditable (including denials).
- Secret redaction is applied consistently to logs/audit/export artifacts.
- Approval (allow-once) works with tight scoping and TTL, and is audited.
- Unit/integration tests cover capability gates, rate limiting, redaction, and approval behavior; E2E covers deny/approve flows.


## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Capability gate matrix: {PromptActive, CommandRunning, AltScreen, RecentGap} × action kinds → expected decision.
  - Approval allow-once scoping (command hash, TTL) and audit output stability.
  - Redaction property tests: secrets never appear in structured logs, audit records, or exported artifacts.

- Integration tests:
  - Wire policy into robot send + workflow send paths and assert:
    - deny returns stable error codes
    - deny/approve decisions are recorded in audit storage

- E2E tests:
  - Deny-by-default scenario for unsafe sends + explicit allow-once override with artifacts (`wa-4vx.10.10`, `wa-4vx.10.16`).

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.8.1","title":"Define policy model: ActionKind, PolicyDecision, authorize() API (capabilities provided separately)","description":"# Task: Define policy model (types + authorize API)

## Goal
Define the *core policy types* and the policy evaluation interface that every action must go through.

This task intentionally focuses on **types and evaluation semantics**, not on how pane state is derived.
Pane capability/state derivation is handled by `wa-4vx.8.8`.

## Why
We want policy decisions that are:
- explicit (stable enums + reasons)
- testable (pure logic)
- portable (CLI/robot/MCP/workflows share one engine)

## Deliverables
- `ActionKind` enum (examples):
  - `SendText`
  - `SendCtrlC`
  - `Spawn`
  - `Split`
  - `Activate`
  - `BrowserAuth`
  - `WorkflowRun`
  - **`ReservePane` / `ReleasePane`**
  - (future) `WriteFile`, etc.
- `PolicyDecision` enum:
  - `Allow`
  - `Deny { reason, rule_id? }`
  - `RequireApproval { reason, rule_id? }`
- `PolicyContext` / `PolicyInput` types that include:
  - actor kind (human|robot|mcp|workflow)
  - target pane_id/domain
  - the derived `PaneCapabilities` snapshot (provided by `wa-4vx.8.8`)
  - optional redacted text summary / action metadata
- `PolicyEngine::authorize(input) -> PolicyDecision` skeleton.

## Non-goals
- Implementing OSC parsing / prompt detection.
- Implementing alt-screen detection.
Those belong in capability/state derivation (`wa-4vx.8.8`).

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - deterministic decisions for synthetic `PaneCapabilities` inputs
  - stable reason strings suitable for robot/MCP

## Acceptance Criteria
- The policy model compiles and is unit-testable with synthetic `PaneCapabilities` inputs.
- Decisions are deterministic and include stable reasons suitable for robot/MCP error envelopes.
